trajectory_summary: This is a short administrative reschedule call. Ty books the appointment and Michael cooperates, but the call does nothing to rebuild the value proposition after a prior no-show, and the button-up is structurally incomplete. The appointment exists on the calendar but is not anchored.
outcome_dispute: No v1 tag is present in the source material. This call should be tagged appointment_rebooked — a prior appointment fell through and a new time was set, but no qualification or value work was done on this touch.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: It should be Matthew that's meeting with you. It might be someone different. It'll get it might get changed tomorrow, so we'll see.
primary_coaching_focus.why: This call is a reschedule of a prior missed appointment — the customer's implicit trust in the process is already strained. Ty introduces closer uncertainty and closes without the verbal handshake, computer anchor, or one-hour block. These are the exact elements that convert a calendar entry into a held sit. The combination of the calendar confusion at 00:42 and the 'so we'll see' at 01:32 means the closer is walking into an appointment with two unaddressed reliability signals and no verbal commitment from Michael.

key_moments.quote: I was just giving you a, call because we had a time set up to share a solar design with you, but that had fallen through. So we were just seeing if there would be a better day, next week that would work for you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ty frames the entire call as a scheduling errand. On a reschedule call — where the customer already no-showed or was missed once — the value proposition needs to be re-lit, not assumed. Ty had a natural opening to briefly re-anchor why the appointment is worth Michael's time before asking for a new slot. He did not take it.

key_moments.quote: Oh, tomorrow is Friday. Yeah. We should be able to do tomorrow for sure. I thought it was Saturday for some reason. My fault.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ty self-corrects a day-of-week error on a call that is already a reschedule of a prior missed appointment. Two scheduling reliability signals in under two minutes — the customer now has implicit evidence that the rep's calendar management is loose. Ty's 'My fault' is honest but does nothing to rebuild confidence.

key_moments.quote: It should be Matthew that's meeting with you. It might be someone different. It'll get it might get changed tomorrow, so we'll see.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ty introduces two layers of uncertainty in the same breath — who the closer will be, and whether that will change. 'So we'll see' is the most trust-eroding phrase in the call. On a reschedule, the customer's implicit question is 'will this actually happen this time?' Ty's answer, unintentionally, is 'maybe not exactly as described.'

key_moments.quote: Looks like he's pretty much open anytime after 1PM, so you can do anytime between one and six.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ty never proactively asked whether anyone else is on the title or needs to be present for the appointment. The third-person reference here ('he's pretty much open') may reflect Ty checking an internal calendar, but it surfaces the absence of any co-decision-maker check across the entire call. On a reschedule touch, this is the natural moment to confirm: 'Is it still just you, or should we make sure your [spouse/partner] is on as well?' That question was never asked.
